title: Do You Need a Visa for Berlin? Quick Summary
items: **There is no separate "Berlin visa."** Berlin is in Germany, which is in the **Schengen Area**, so Germany's entry rules are your Berlin rules.

**EU, EEA and Swiss visitors:** the Schengen 90/180 cap does not apply. For a short visit, bring a valid passport or national ID card; stays beyond three months can involve residence conditions and registration.

**Visa status depends on your passport and documents.** Common visa-exempt passports include the US, UK, Canada, Australia, Japan and South Korea; other travellers normally need a **Schengen short-stay visa**. Residence permits, long-stay visas and family status can change the answer.

**The 90/180 rule is genuinely rolling.** Each day of a visa-free short stay must fit within **90 days in the preceding 180**, across all Schengen countries. The checker below uses exact previous entry and exit dates, not one total.

**EES is now live (since 10 April 2026).** It covers most non-EU nationals making a short stay, with exemptions. Visa-free travellers normally register a face and four fingerprints; short-stay visa holders have their face stored in EES while their fingerprints remain in VIS.

**ETIAS is not required yet.** It is due in the **last quarter of 2026**, followed by transitional and grace periods lasting at least 12 months in total. Applications are not open today; the future fee is **20 euros**.
**Check your passport first.** It should be valid for at least **three months beyond departure** and issued within the last **ten years**. That is the most common avoidable problem.
